home.title: Noctis — VLESS Browser Extension for Chrome | Browser Proxy (VMess, Trojan, Shadowsocks)
home.description: Free Chrome extension to connect to VLESS, VMess, Trojan, Shadowsocks, Hysteria2 and Reality proxies. Powered by sing-box (Xray/V2Ray-compatible). No system VPN — proxy stays in the browser.
home.hero.h1_sub: Route browser traffic through your own proxies
home.hero.lede: Noctis is a free browser extension that routes Chrome through VLESS, VMess, Trojan, Shadowsocks, Hysteria2, Reality and other proxy servers via a local helper powered by sing-box. No system VPN, no separate client window — proxying stays inside the browser.
home.feat.servers.title: Servers from share links, QR, or subscription URLs
home.feat.servers.body: Paste vless://, vmess://, trojan://, ss://, hysteria2://, tuic://, wireguard:// — or scan a QR code. Subscription URLs auto-refresh on a schedule.
home.feat.routing.title: Per-rule routing
home.feat.routing.body: Match by domain, GeoSite, or GeoIP. Each rule routes to proxy, direct, or block.
home.feat.modes.title: Three routing modes
home.feat.modes.body: Global sends everything through the proxy. Rules only routes matches. Direct bypasses entirely.
home.feat.health.title: Health checks + automatic failover
home.feat.health.body: Background latency probes; one-tap manual ping per server. Failing servers drop out of the active route.
home.feat.pinned.title: Pinned-server shortlist
home.feat.pinned.body: Keep three favorites at the top of the popup. Switch active server without opening the full panel.
home.feat.logs.title: Live log stream
home.feat.logs.body: sing-box stdout and stderr stream into the extension. Diagnose connection issues without leaving the browser.
home.feat.webrtc.title: WebRTC leak guard
home.feat.webrtc.body: Optional toggle blocks UDP outside the proxy so WebRTC can't reveal your real IP.
home.feat.adblock.title: Bundled ad and tracker block rules
home.feat.adblock.body: geosite:ads families route to block by default. Toggle off if you prefer to handle it elsewhere.
home.protocols.body: Noctis supports every transport sing-box exposes: VLESS (including VLESS Reality), VMess, Trojan, Shadowsocks, Hysteria2, TUIC, WireGuard, AnyTLS and ShadowTLS. Configs from V2Ray, Xray and 3X-UI panels work as-is — paste a share link or subscription URL and the extension translates it into a sing-box outbound automatically.
home.why.body: Browsers can't run a sing-box engine on their own. Three pieces split the work across the sandbox boundary — and the arrow that crosses it is the only place messages flow.
home.singbox.h2: What is sing-box, and why Noctis uses it
home.singbox.body: sing-box is the open-source proxy engine that runs underneath Noctis. It's compatible with V2Ray and Xray configurations and supports VLESS, VMess, Trojan, Shadowsocks, Hysteria2, TUIC, Reality, AnyTLS, ShadowTLS and WireGuard out of the box. Noctis bundles a small native helper that supervises sing-box on your machine, so the browser extension only has to send routing decisions — never raw traffic.
home.start.body: Install the extension from the Chrome Web Store, then run the helper installer for your OS. Add a server, route your traffic. The status badge turns green when sing-box accepts your first connection.
home.faq.what.q: What is VLESS and why use it in a browser?
home.faq.what.a: VLESS is a lightweight proxy protocol from the V2Ray/Xray family. It carries no encryption of its own — TLS does that — so it's fast and easy to disguise as ordinary HTTPS. Using VLESS through a browser extension means only browser traffic is proxied; the rest of your operating system stays on your real connection.
home.faq.vpn.q: How is a browser proxy extension different from a VPN?
home.faq.vpn.a: A VPN tunnels every app on your system through one connection and usually needs admin rights. A browser proxy extension like Noctis only routes the browser, requires no root or admin, and lets you keep Zoom, Steam, Telegram desktop and torrents on your real network at the same time.
home.faq.reality.a: Yes. VLESS Reality is a standard sing-box outbound, and Noctis passes Reality parameters (Server Name, Fingerprint, SNI, Dest, public key, short ID) through to the helper unchanged. Paste a vless://...flow=xtls-rprx-vision&security=reality share link and the extension imports every field.
home.faq.protocols.q: Which proxy protocols does Noctis support?
home.faq.protocols.a: VLESS, VMess, Trojan, Shadowsocks, Hysteria2, TUIC, WireGuard, AnyTLS and ShadowTLS — anything sing-box supports as an outbound. V2Ray and Xray share links work as-is.
home.faq.safe.q: Is a Chrome proxy extension safe to use?
home.faq.safe.a: Safer than most. Noctis sends nothing to its developer — no analytics, no telemetry, no remote config. Server configs stay in browser storage. The native helper runs without admin rights. The full permission list and rationale is on the privacy page.
home.faq.platforms.q: Does Noctis work on Windows, macOS and Linux?
home.faq.platforms.a: Yes — Chromium-based browsers on Windows, macOS and Linux (Chrome, Edge, Brave, Arc, Vivaldi, Opera, Yandex Browser). The native helper has one-line install scripts for each platform.
home.faq.subscription.q: Can I use a subscription URL to auto-update servers?
home.faq.subscription.a: Yes. Paste a subscription URL once and Noctis refreshes it on a schedule. Server lists update automatically; pinned and active selections survive refreshes.
home.faq.bypass.q: Will Noctis help bypass website blocks?
home.faq.bypass.a: Noctis itself is just a proxy client — it routes your browser through whatever server you provide. If your server is in a region where the site you want to reach is accessible, Noctis routes you there. It does not provide servers; you supply them.
home.faq.webrtc.a: Yes. An optional toggle blocks UDP outside the proxy so WebRTC can't reveal your real IP while the proxy is active.
home.faq.cost.a: Free. The extension is free in the Chrome Web Store and the native helper is open-source under MIT. You only pay for the proxy servers you choose to use.
install.title: Install Noctis — VLESS Chrome Extension Setup (Windows, macOS, Linux)
install.description: Step-by-step install guide for the Noctis VLESS browser extension: Chrome Web Store install, native sing-box helper setup for Windows, macOS, Linux. One-line install scripts.
install.lede: The Noctis extension needs a small native helper running on your machine. The helper supervises sing-box, the engine that actually does the proxying.
install.before.title: Before you start
install.before.browser: A Chromium-based browser, version 120 or newer (Chrome, Chromium, Edge, Brave, Arc, Vivaldi, Opera, Yandex Browser).
install.before.disk: About 50 MB of free disk for the helper and sing-box.
install.before.admin: No admin / root rights — everything installs into your user account.
install.step1.title: 1. Install the extension
install.step1.body: Install Noctis from the Chrome Web Store. Open the extension after install — it will detect that the helper is missing and show a setup dialog with a one-liner pre-filled for your machine.
install.step2.title: 2. Run the helper installer
install.step2.body1: Copy the command from the extension's Helper Setup dialog and paste it into your terminal. Your extension ID is already filled in — you don't need to look it up. For reference, the command looks like this:
install.step2.body2: The installer downloads noctis-host and sing-box into your user data directory and writes a native-messaging manifest for every supported browser.
install.step2.body3: The first time the extension talks to the helper, your browser may show a one-time native-messaging prompt — approve it.
install.step3.title: 3. First run
install.step3.body: Open the extension's popup, paste a vless://, ss://, or trojan:// share link (or a subscription URL), and toggle the active server. The status badge turns green once sing-box accepts traffic.
install.updating.title: Updating
install.updating.body: Rerun the one-liner for your OS — the script is idempotent and will replace the existing binaries.
install.uninstalling.title: Uninstalling
privacy.title: Privacy Policy — Noctis Browser Proxy Extension
privacy.description: What Noctis does not collect: no traffic logs, no telemetry, no analytics. Server configs stay in browser storage. Full Chrome permission breakdown and rationale.
privacy.lede: Noctis is built to do one thing — route your browser's traffic through proxy servers you have configured. This page explains what data Noctis touches, what it does with it, and what it explicitly does not do.
privacy.stores.intro: The extension stores the following inside your browser's local extension storage (chrome.storage.local):
privacy.stores.item1: The list of proxy servers you have added.
privacy.stores.item4: UI preferences (theme, side panel pin, log verbosity, etc.).
privacy.stores.outro: The native helper (noctis-host) writes a sing-box configuration file under your operating-system user-data directory. This file mirrors the same servers and rules.
privacy.network.h2: What Noctis sends over the network
privacy.network.intro: The only network traffic Noctis itself generates is:
privacy.network.proxied.body: — your browser's requests, routed through the proxy servers you configured. Noctis does not pick destinations; you do.
privacy.network.sub.body: — if you add a subscription URL, Noctis periodically fetches that URL to update the server list it represents. No subscription URL is added unless you add it.
privacy.network.ip.body: — only while connected, an on-demand request to api.ipify.org that travels through your active proxy so the popup can show your visible exit IP. It confirms the proxy is working; it does not run when you are disconnected, and it never reveals your real IP to that service.
privacy.network.outro: That's it. Noctis has no analytics SDK, no crash reporter, no remote configuration endpoint, no opt-in or opt-out telemetry, and no account system.
privacy.nothing.item1: It does not send your browsing history, server list, settings, or any identifier to the developer. The only third-party request it makes is the optional IP check above, which rides your active proxy and exposes your exit IP — never your real one.
privacy.nothing.item2: It does not embed third-party trackers or fingerprinting libraries.
privacy.nothing.item3: It does not require a sign-up or login.
privacy.nothing.item4: It does not write to disk outside the helper's user-data directory (sing-box config + logs you can read yourself).
privacy.perm.proxy: Tell Chrome to use sing-box's local SOCKS listener when a routing profile is active.
privacy.perm.storage: Persist your servers, rules, and settings inside the browser.
privacy.perm.nativeMessaging: The only channel by which the extension talks to the local helper.
privacy.perm.privacy: Required by the Chrome proxy API surface for proxy-setting scope.
privacy.perm.alarms: Run periodic health checks and refresh subscriptions on a schedule.
privacy.perm.tabs: Side panel and per-tab status context.
privacy.perm.dnr: Block ad/tracker domains per active routing profile.
privacy.perm.hosts: Routing rules match arbitrary destinations; per-URL proxy decisions require visibility into the URL.
privacy.children.body: Noctis is not designed for or directed at children under 13. It collects no information from anyone.
privacy.changes.body: Material changes will be published here with a new "Last updated" date. The store listing will be updated to match.
